entries.ocr_text:
FOREWORD
The 1954 General Assembly imposed upon the Legislative
Research Commission the duty of furnishing the printer correct
copy of bills and resolutions for inclusion in the Session Acts .
Section 57.180 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes stipulates that
bound copies of Session Acts be delivered for distribution within
sixty days after the end of a session .
In the preparation of this volume of the 1959 Extraordinary
Session Acts the Secretary of State has furnished access to copy
of filed enrolled bills and resolutions . The staff of the Legislative
Research Commission compared the prepared page proofs with
the enrolled bills . Mr. Robert K. Main on the research staff of
the Commission has prepared this volume under the supervision
of James T. Fleming , Executive Assistant .
WILSON W. WYATT
Chairman
Legislative Research Commission
Frankfort , Kentucky
January 20, 1960
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1959, p. 6

KENTUCKY , ON TUESDAY , DECEMBER THE TWENTY -SECOND,
1959 , AND ENDED WEDNESDAY , DECEMBER THE THIRTIETH ,
1959 .
CHAPTER 1
(H. R. 6 )
A JOINT RESOLUTION appropriating money for the 1959 Extraordinary
Session of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky .
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky :
Section 1. That there is hereby appropriated out of the
General Expenditure Fund for the payment of salaries and other
expenses of the 1959 Extraordinary Session of the General As-
sembly the sum of seventy-five thousand ( $75,000 ) dollars .
Section 2. That whereas funds heretofore appropriated for
sessions of the General Assembly are exhausted, an emergency is
declared to exist and this resolution shall become effective im-
mediately upon
itspassage and approval by the Governor

entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1959, p. 11

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 258 of the Constitution of Kentucky
classification.summary: This act outlines the process for amending the Kentucky Constitution. It requires a majority vote in two consecutive General Assemblies to call a convention for revising or amending the Constitution, with specific limitations on the subjects that can be considered. The act details the voting procedures and certification requirements for such a convention. It explicitly states that no changes can be made to the Bill of Rights.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the legislative process for calling a convention to amend the Kentucky Constitution. It outlines voting requirements, certification procedures, and limitations on the scope of amendments. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the text clearly describes a constitutional amendment process without any racial component. Human review is not needed.

258 of the present Constitution shall take effect and become
operative , the same shall be submitted to the qualified voters of
this Commonwealth , after at least ninety days' notice , and rati-
fied by a majority of those voting .
Approved December 30 , 1959
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1959, p. 12

classification.title: An Act relating to the submission and ratification of a new Constitution.
classification.summary: This act outlines the process for submitting a new Constitution, agreed upon by a convention, to the qualified voters of Kentucky for ratification. It specifies that at least ninety days' notice must be given before the vote, and the Constitution must be ratified by a majority of those voting.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is an excerpt from an act passed in 1959 that deals with the procedure for ratifying a new state constitution. It lists various topics that might be included in such a constitution, such as the judicial department, compensation of public officers, and the order of succession for the Governor. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices typically associated with Jim Crow laws. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.
